Hi Moon,

I don't see the .log file- maybe it failed to attach?

Additionally, when running dRep, it's necessary to specify an output directory, but not with the `-o` argument. So the command could be something like:

`dRep dereplicate filter -g *.fa`

Have the `-o` might be causing the problem? Additionally, I would encourage you to use an up-to-date  version of the program (`pip install drep --upgrade`). More recent versions use `dereplicate` instead of `dereplicate_wf`.

If none of these suggestions work, please either try attaching the log file again through GitHub or email me.

Best,
-Matt

Supervisor has an option for `stopsignal` - usually that's `TERM` (`SIGTERM`) but you can set it to `QUIT`, or in my case, `INT` (`SIGINT` which correlates to ^C). `INT` works for Bender, and setting the `stopwaitsecs` option lets you give bender time to shut things down before rebooting.

Thanks for pointing this out. However, I think that apart from an annoying warning message this is harmless.

In your example where ``self.models.bounds`` returns ``[(None, None), (None, None), (None, None)]``, it could have been replaced by just ``None``. But in the case where you set even just one, it has to be e.g. ``[(0.0, None), (None, None), (None, None)]``.

Every line in the code of ``scipy slsqp`` is fine with this, except the offending line 341. The end result is that the `None` are always replaced by `NaN` before being passed to the Fortran code _in both scenarios_ of the `if ... else ...`. 

I agree that this warning doesn't actually provide any useful information, but I'm not sure we should just catch it in `symfit`. Maybe this is something to fix in `scipy` (or there is a reason they didn't).

Assuming that my remote is called "upstream", the easiest way is to do a
"git reset --soft upstream/master" to uncommit all of the files that you've
added since my last known commit, and then just commit those back in as one
big commit. You can also do an interactive rebase (e.g. git rebase -i
HEAD~5) and just ask to squash your last three commits at the top into your
first commit and then just adjust the commit message when the editor opens.

If you're scared of making a mistake, just note your current tip (latest)
revision and then, if you have issues, do a reset-hard (e.g. git reset
--hard <revision>) to set your tip back to it. All of the information will
still be there for a bit.

I'm not so keen on removing `.get()` as a property accessor. I am fine with it going at the end of the list and preferring dict-access and attribute access. I think `.get()` is least used of those patterns in Python, however I dunno if there is a compelling reason to remove support for it. I think the handlebars pattern of allowing access to pretty much any sort of data container is useful.

Hi! Yep, if you load the previous session with `zap-cli session load /path/to/saved-session`, then you can simply run `zap-cli spider <URL to spider>` again. Thanks for the detailed response, I always learn something new when reading them :+1: 

Regarding handling `SystemError`, I understand that the python interpreter might be in an inconsistent state after it, but what if `pebble` catches the exception, tries to send it to the main thread, and then kills the process (which is in the inconsistent state).

That way the `pebble` user can:
 * Know that the task failed and retry, ignore, stop the whole process, etc.
 * Pebble is not in an inconsistent state, since the process was killed

I'm not sure about other programs, but identifying that this `SystemError` (which is only displayed on stderr and can't be handled by the program) is difficult: the error doesn't appear in the logs, can't catch it with try/except, etc.

Another question, do you also believe that `MemoryError` shouldn't be handled?

Otherwise one might as well just calculate the sys_path to store it in their IDE of choice and send it with the regular completion/goto-definition requests.

Another alternative would be to make Script objects stateful, enable changing source piecewise (e.g. `script.set_source(begin, end, text)` and run completion as `completions(line, column)`. But something tells me that avoiding state was one of the initial design decisions.

I propose the following features, feel free to comment, add or remove:

1) Add default limits thresholds:

```
[dockers]
# Thresthold for CPU (in %)
cpu_careful=50
cpu_warning=70
cpu_critical=90
# Thresthold for MEM (in MB)
mem_careful=20
mem_warning=50
mem_critical=70

2) Define per container limits thresholds. Container identification is done by the container name:

For example, to define limits for a container named *dockerinfluxdbgrafana_influxdb_1*:

```
[dockers]
# Thresthold for CPU (in %)

dockerinfluxdbgrafana_influxdb_1_cpu_warning=15
dockerinfluxdbgrafana_influxdb_1_cpu_critical=20

dockerinfluxdbgrafana_influxdb_1_cpu_critical_action=echo {{name}} {{Image}} {{Id}} {{cpu}} > /tmp/dockerinfluxdbgrafana_influxdb_1.alert

If you're using a custom theme, make sure to include the following definition:

```css
.filtered { display: none; }
```

If you need to alter that class name, then set the [`filter_filteredRow` widgetOption](https://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/#widget-filter-filteredrow).

I did not notice any characters in other locale regexes that would cause this kind of problem, but it is admittedly a bit difficult to distinguish in some cases what is used as a regex versus a regular string. It would be helpful for 3.0 to document how the strings are used. For example, `Modifiers` is used both as a regex and as a string so if you specified `'foo|bar': -1` it would match either *foo* or *bar*, but then when used as a string lookup, neither *foo* or *bar* are keys in the `Modifiers` dict. It may also be important to avoid adding groups. In the French locale (#185) I noticed a lot of `(é|e)` which should either be `(?:é|e)`, `[ée]`, or split into entirely separate strings with no regex alternation if the value is used for plain string matching.

In most cases it's not terribly difficult to figure these out but better documentation would reduce the barrier to adding locales.

I was curious to see if there was a significant difference in speed between the two approaches, so I did a quick informal test using a CSV file with 100,000 rows ...

```
1,record000000
2,record000001
3,record000002
...
100000,record099999
```

... inserting into an empty table on a remote SQL Server instance.

The times for `bcp` to do the insert were highly variable, ranging from 7 seconds to 32 seconds.

For `fast_executemany` to do the insert, i.e.,

```python
cnxn = pyodbc.connect(conn_str, autocommit=False)
crsr = cnxn.cursor()
crsr.fast_executemany = True
t0 = time.time()
data = []
with open(r'C:\Users\Gord\Desktop\test10k.csv', newline='') as csvfile:
    rdr = csv.reader(csvfile, delimiter=',', quoting=csv.QUOTE_NONE)
    data = [(int(row[0]), row[1]) for row in rdr]
sql = 'INSERT INTO test10k (id, txt) VALUES (?,?)'
crsr.executemany(sql, data)
cnxn.commit()
print(f"{(time.time() - t0):.1f} seconds")
```

the times were more consistent, ranging from 20 seconds to 30 seconds.

So, `fast_executemany` may turn out to be slower than `bcp`, but perhaps not outrageously so.

Hi!

It turns out that the answer is: yes, you can do this, but it's a bit sneaky.

The `options` argument that can be passed into `create_table` is a dictionary, and those key-values are set as attributes on the class it creates. This works for things like `allow_sort` and `allow_empty` etc, but also works for defining methods, because as far as Python is concerned, they are basically the same.

For example, the table in [the `sortable.py` example](https://github.com/plumdog/flask_table/blob/master/examples/sortable.py) is the same as:
```python
def dynamic_sort_url(self, col_key, reverse=False):
    if reverse:
        direction = 'desc'
    else:
        direction = 'asc'
    return url_for('index', sort=col_key, direction=direction)

# alternatively, if you like using lambda
dynamic_sort_url = lambda self, col_key, reverse=False: url_for('index', sort=col_key, direction='desc' if reverse else 'asc')

SortableTable = (
    create_table(
        'SortableTable',
        options=dict(
            allow_sort=True,
            sort_url=dynamic_sort_url))
    .add_column('id', Col('ID'))
    .add_column('name', Col('Description'))
    .add_column('link', LinkCol('Link', 'flask_link', url_kwargs=dict(id='id'), allow_sort=False))
)
```

Which works, but is maybe a bit on the ugly side.

Perhaps a neater way would be:

```python
class SortableTableBase(Table):
    def sort_url(self, col_key, reverse=False):
        if reverse:
            direction = 'desc'
        else:
            direction = 'asc'
        return url_for('index', sort=col_key, direction=direction)

SortableTable = (
    create_table(
        'SortableTable',
        base=SortableTableBase,  # A custom base class for the dynamic table
        options=dict(
            allow_sort=True))
    .add_column('id', Col('ID'))
    .add_column('name', Col('Description'))
    .add_column('link', LinkCol('Link', 'flask_link', url_kwargs=dict(id='id'), allow_sort=False))
)
```

Hopefully one of those will work for you, and maybe you can see from the examples above why each one works (if you copy the contents of the `sortable.py` example and copy in my code snippets from above to replace the table, hopefully they'll work).

And so it turns out that this is absolutely *not* a greenhorn question - we're in Metaclass-land here. And this could really do with being made clearer in the docs, so I'll add that.

Thanks for your question! I hope my answer helps.

Currently, the RayTracing class has a class PlaneCoordinates, that represents the coordinates used for ray-tracing. This is messy, as the different sets of coordinates we use (image, sub, sparse, etc.) are being explicitly passed around the RayTracing class, when the class itself shouldn't care what coordinates we are actually ray-tracing.

The Planes that we set up however, do care, as to generate a model image from a light profile or source-plane pixelization we will be using multiple grids.

Therefore, I am going to do away with the PlaneCoordinates class, and in the image module have a class called AnalysisGrids. AnalysisGrids stores all the 2D coordinate grids (after translation to 1D) that we use for lens modeling. Thus, we pass RayTracing a set of AnalysisGrids, and it automatically performs ray-tracing on them, but the specifics of the grid types are irrelevent to RayTracing. The Plane classes are however set up with the complete set of AnalysisGrids, which have suitably been ray-tracing beforehand.

I think we should allow passing a coverage.py XML file to Vulture. Instead of reporting all unused code, we can then collect a list of false-positives, i.e, items that Vulture detects as unused but which are reported as used in the XML file (since we know the filename and line number for all items that Vulture detects as unused). Then we can output the false-positives in a format suitable for adding to a whitelist file.

Some implementation notes: Let's call the new parameter `--make-whitelist COVERAGE_XML_FILE` and use the following format for the whitelist, `myfunc  # foo.py:42: unused function 'myfunc'`.

Hi egormin!

If you're seeing this, then the passwords *must* be stored somewhere in plain text, on disk, at some time. That's not a good idea. You should use SSH keys or type in the password manually when connecting. For other non-connect secrets (such as application or user passwords) you should store them using ansible vault (http://docs.ansible.com/ansible/2.4/vault.html).

It's not possible for ansible-cmdb to guess which of the variables are sensitive and which aren't. If it's available in plain text, then it should be assumed that it's not sensitive (otherwise, why would it be available in plain text?).

So I'm afraid I can't fix this, and I suggest you look at how it's possible that this information ended up in Ansible-cmdb and fix the issue there. I think SSH keys are the solution here. Not only are they much more secure, they're also easier to use. There's a nice tutorial available (here)[https://www.digitalocean.com/community/tutorials/ssh-essentials-working-with-ssh-servers-clients-and-keys]

With kind regards,

Ferry

Okay, I'll remove the return types, at least.

Perhaps rather than be an instance method, you could instead provide this functionality via a `from_attributes` alternate constructor?

So instead of writing:

    new_obj = existing_obj.replace(field1=value1)

you'd instead write:

    new_obj = MyDataClass.from_attributes(existing_obj, field1=value1)

One nice aspect of this approach is that the return type is locally explicit - you know `new_obj` will be an instance of `MyDataClass`, regardless of the exact type of `existing_obj`. If you want the return type to be dynamic, you can do that explicitly:

    new_obj = type(existing_obj).from_attributes(existing_obj, field1=value1)

@fvlima I've been thinking about this issue and I've come to the conclusion that using generators only avoids the converstion to iterable that's inevitable on most cases. That's because models were designed to be used/accessed many times, and generators aren't the best choice for this case.

```python

import typing

from simple_model import Model


class Product(Model):
    title: str


class ShoppingCart(Model):
    items = typing.List[Products]


products_data = [{'title': 'Cool Gadget #' + i} for i in range(1, 11)]
products = Product.build_many(products_data)  # returns generator. the slow conversion is avoided

cart = ShoppingCart(items=products)
cart.items  # generator
cart.clean()  # conversion happens here
cart.validate()  # or here
dict(cart)  # here too
cart.items = list(cart.items)  # maybe here (more complicated to use)
```

Generators really shine when used on for loops (or are consumed step by step once). For example, the `range` and `reversed` built-in functions are used, on most cases, on for loops and aren't used again. The same doesn't apply to models and its fields.

In fact, the `Model.build_many` classmethod was implemented to solve problems such as the example above where at some point the conversion will happen.

But I believe your suggestion is valid and there might be cases (IMHO not most cases though) where using generators is the solution with best performance. So for these specific circumstances we may use the `model_many_builder` [1] over `Model.build_many`. The `model_many_builder` was implemented to return a list, but I believe it should be changed to return generators. Its usage would bee something like:

```python

from simple_model.builder import model_many_builder

# using previous example objects
products = model_many_builder(products_data, cls=Product)
for product in products:  # generator
    heavy_operation(product)  # something slow or "unreliable" as making requests to external APIs
```

Things you did so far is just correct, I've loaded the json content in your comment and logging the errors:
```
2017-02-01 07:54:05,787 - pyswagger.core - ERROR - ((u'#/apis/default/apis/updateFile/parameters/3', 'Parameter'), 'body parameter with invalid name: fileUpload')
2017-02-01 07:54:05,787 - pyswagger.core - ERROR - ((u'#/apis/default/apis/uploadFile/parameters/3', 'Parameter'), 'body parameter with invalid name: fileUpload')
```

In short, the provide name "fileUpload" for body parameters, which is meaningless and prohibited by OpenAPI 1.2 spec ([ref](https://github.com/OAI/OpenAPI-Specification/blob/master/versions/1.2.md#524-parameter-object)). But it should be just fine to use this spec by skipping the validation step of pyswagger, by this:
```python
# instead of calling App.prepare

About #403, this issue is validating before being merged in the master branch but as you say, we do not support Pythran module imported in another Pythran module. I think there are few reasons for this:
1) As it is compiled, with module `a` loading module `b` and module `c` loading module `b`, if you update `b` for something in `a`, it has some risk to break your module `b`. (Well it is not a really good argument because it may happen in Python too)
2) If we import Pythran module, it will disable some inter-procedural optimizations. (Ok, it is user choices...)
3) How can we specify if we want to import the Python module or the Pythran one? (I think this is the only real blocking issue)
4) The import of custom modules is very recent in the master branch of Pythran so we didn't have enough feedback to continue on it. But hopefully, you are here :-)

django-sequences is really designed to generate **gapless** sequences. This is a regulatory requirement in some contexts (e.g. accounting). These tend not to be write-heavy contexts, or at least not concurrent-write-heavy contexts.

This causes PostgreSQL to do a lot of work to synchronize between connections attempting to increment the counter. This is where the overhead comes from.

If you don't need gapless sequences, you can probably find a less expensive solution (in terms of database load), perhaps involving PostgreSQL counters.

If you do need gapless sequences, then you need either a database that's fast enough to handle the transactional workload or another solution for generating IDs.

It's not meant to be combined.
The API model describe how you serialize an object whatever it is, a mongoengine object or a peewee one. You simply return it in your API method.
```python
todo_fields = api.model('Todo', {
   'id': fields.Integer(readonly=True),
   'title': fields.String(required=True),
 })

class TodoResource(Resource):
    @api.expect(todo_fields)
    @api.marshal_with(todo_fields)
    def get(self):
        # Using MongoEngine, I can write:
        todo = Todo.objects.get(id=1)
        return todo
```

It's tempting to have the same models between your ORM and your API but sometime:
- you won't return all the fields
- you won't return a DB model
- you will apply some transformation
- documentations will differs or be insufficient
- ...

If you need to have exactly the same fields, you can write a piece of code transforming a peewee or a mongoengine model into an API model.

`<authPassed>` containing a value of `0` indicates that you didn't provide a valid session id (`sid`).

Monitor the requests in your browser again, but look for `&sid=` in the URL.  It should have some random letters/numbers after it like this: `&sid=846w45fh`

Append that to the end of `/cgi-bin/management/manaRequest.cgi?subfunc=sysinfo&hd=no&multicpu=1` like so:

```
/cgi-bin/management/manaRequest.cgi?subfunc=sysinfo&hd=no&multicpu=1&sid=846w45fh
```

You should end up with a longer response.

If I understand your question correctly, it seems like you're asking more of a Python question as opposed to a python-cloudant library question.  A python-cloudant Document object is a sub-class of `dict` so when you retrieve your Cloudant document content as a python-cloudant Document object you can access that content as you would from a `dict`.

So to answer your question, you can retrieve the attachment name(s) for your attachment(s) by using any valid Python `dict`-like retrieval function.

@jeffreypicard Thanks for taking this on. I had planned at some point to work on this, and as often happens, got swamped. 

I've spoken to a few different companies that use `trigger_dag` and `TriggerDagRunOperator` and one common use-case is to handle high-volume event traffic. One company has thousands of Kafka consumers, each of which run a Sensing DAG that has a custom Kafka sensor. When an event arrives, each Sensing DAG executes a TriggerDagRunOperator which will result in work being done via the processing dag (e.g. https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/airflow-lesson-1-triggerdagrunoperator-siddharth-anand?trk=prof-post).

Hence, the cli is one part of the Trigger DAG use-case, the other being the TriggerDagRunOperator.

Hence, to make this work correctly, it seems that you should also ensure the TriggerDagRunOperator is passed an execution date. The one area I might be concerned about, mostly out of ignorance, is whether there will be any unique index violations because of some expectation of granularity of time.

Finally, I had created 2 examples dags in `example_trigger_target_dag.py` and `example_trigger_controller_dag.py`. Feel free to add to those examples. Also, I don't recall if I added tests.. that might be good to add for your new functionality.

Hi @lfiaschi ,

The API always returns 100 activities for one API call, and we do auto-fetching for paginated response in the Cursor object. You can see detailed implementation in https://github.com/facebook/facebook-python-ads-sdk/blob/master/facebookads/api.py#L687

This means that if you loop over the Cursor, you'll get the right number of total activities. What happens is that it will automatically send another API call to fetch more objects if you have reached the end of the current queue (100 activities). However, this also means the len() function of Cursor only reflects the number of objects in the current queue, and it does not correctly reflect the total number of activities.

To give some background on why this is happening in boto3, this is because of how the IAM service is returning data to us.  In the case of the `GroupDetailList`, empty group policy lists look like this over the wire (the raw HTTP response that IAM sends back):

```
<GroupDetailList>
  <member>
    <Path>/</Path>
    <GroupName>TestGroup</GroupName>
    <AttachedManagedPolicies>
      <member>
        <PolicyArn>...</PolicyArn>
        <PolicyName>AdministratorAccess</PolicyName>
      </member>
    </AttachedManagedPolicies>
    <GroupId>asdf</GroupId>
    <Arn>arn:aws:iam::asdf:group/TestGroup</Arn>
    <GroupPolicyList/>            <--- Note here the empty GroupPolicyList element
    <CreateDate>2017-10-10T20:20:58Z</CreateDate>
  </member>

```

Compared to what IAM sends back for the user details list:

```
<UserDetailList>
  <member>
    <Path>/</Path>
    <AttachedManagedPolicies>
      <member>
        <PolicyArn>...</PolicyArn>
        <PolicyName>AdministratorAccess</PolicyName>
      </member>
    </AttachedManagedPolicies>
    <GroupList/>
    <UserName>admin</UserName>
    <Arn>arn:aws:iam::asdf:user/admin</Arn>
    <UserId>asdf</UserId>
    <CreateDate>2017-10-10T18:11:22Z</CreateDate>
  </member>

```

So if the service returns an empty `<GroupPolicyList/>`, then we parse that as an empty list: `"GroupPolicyList": []`.  However, if the key is omitted, as it is in the above XML response for `UserDetailList`, then the key is omitted from our parsed result.  In other words, boto3 is producing different results because this is what the IAM service is returning to us.

The best way to get this updated is for the service team is to return a `<UserPolicyList/>` in their response.  This would ensure that all SDKs parse this value consistently.  I'd suggest asking on the [IAM forums](https://forums.aws.amazon.com/forum.jspa?forumID=76) to see if the service team can get this updated.

Let me know if you have any more questions.

Hi @tianhao64, thank you very much for the inclusion of the SMS API in pyvmomi.
To use the SMS API using sms.ServiceInstance, the user must instantiate the class with a SoapStubAdapter. It is mandatory that this adapter send an additional SOAP header named vcSessionCookie for authentication.
It is currently trivial to pass this SOAP header and not possible to do that without affecting all stub adapters in the thread: the code that adds SOAP headers is in https://github.com/vmware/pyvmomi/blob/master/pyVmomi/SoapAdapter.py#L874, and from here we can see that the user must change `_threadLocalContext['reqCtx']`. This is not good because it requires changing an internal global variable and also affects the thread context, which means that the regular clients are affected to.
Please see the discussion and commit in https://github.com/vmware/pyvmomi/pull/165.

Thanks. For everyone facing the same Problem, this is a workaround while this issue is not fixed:

```python
from paypalrestsdk.resource import List, Find, Create, Post, Replace

class PatchablePayment(List, Find, Create, Post, Replace):
    """
    The Paypal-SDK does not provide replace operations 
    for payment resources. These are neccessary for the 
    patch-call. Thus, we implement our own payment resource.
    See https://github.com/paypal/PayPal-Python-SDK/issues/139
    for updates and a possible resolution for this workaround
    """
    path = "v1/payments/payment"
    def execute(self, attributes):
        return self.post('execute', attributes, self)
```

Just use this class instead of the normal Payment-Class, e.g. like this:
```python
resource = PatchablePayment.find(paypal_id)
resource.replace(operations)
```

```
jtratner@C13101jtratner$ (git::master) source activate
(root) jtratner@C13101jtratner$ (git::master) which python
/Users/jtratner/miniconda3/bin/python

similar to virtualenv:

```
jtratner@C13101jtratner$ (git::master) source activate myenv

The core trade-off I'm aware is that using multiple files makes it much easier to avoid conflicts when multiple processes are writing to the cache at the same time, but also uses up more inodes on the filesystem (which is typically only a potential problem on actual file servers, not development workstations).

There are also differences in how the two models interact with filesystem caching in general, as well as what happens when the cache is actually stored on a network drive.

Unfortunately, I've never had much luck looking for actual guides on this, since my search results always get swamped by links related to regular application caching.

However, one use-case-specific consideration here is that it would be nice if the `pipenv` artifact metadata caches used a similar layout to `pip`'s actual artifact caches, so if you know how to navigate one, you know how to navigate the other. That would push towards a multi-file storage layout.

@ja89son @Archer456

The path `build/` is not hard-coded.  If one runs `./setup.py build -b temp` the build artifacts are placed in `temp/` instead of `build/`.

Now, I just tested the setup.py under master with this, and it does still work because all the Cython output is currently hard-coded to go into `build/`.  And it's rare that anyone will use this option, but it's still best to to not assume.  Also confusing is that under `build/` some files are going into `build/cysignals` while others are going into `build/src/cysignals`.

For Cython I find it easier to deal with if the generated files are created alongside their associated pyx and pyd files.  For one it makes debugging a bit easier--it's not uncommon to have to look at the .c files to see what Cython is outputting, and it's annoying if they're placed in a parallel but separate "source" tree.  It's worse for generated header files, because now you have headers coming from multiple directories instead of all being in one place.  Finally, it makes it easier (though this is more a limitation of distutils than a real technical barrier) to include the generated C sources in the source distribution.  This makes it easier to distribute the package without requiring the person installing to have Cython (or a new enough Cython for that matter).

I sympathize with preferring to keep build artifacts completely segregated and in other cases I'd agree, but I've found that dealing with Cython is much easier if that's not the case.  I've yet to see a good argument for how the end result is different in any practical sense, especially if the generated files are consistently ignored by git.

A colleague had the following suggestion as to the cause
```
On unix systems (or on Windows using cygwin or mingw+msys) the command typically looks something like this:

    gcc shell.c sqlite3.c -lpthread -ldl

The pthreads library is needed to make SQLite threadsafe. But since the CLI is single threaded, we could instruct SQLite to build in a non-threadsafe mode and thereby omit the pthreads library:

    gcc -DSQLITE_THREADSAFE=0 shell.c sqlite3.c -ldl

The -ldl library is needed to support dynamic loading, the sqlite3_load_extension() interface and the load_extension() SQL function. If these features are not required, then they can be omitted using SQLITE_OMIT_LOAD_EXTENSION compile-time option:

    gcc -DSQLITE_THREADSAFE=0 -DSQLITE_OMIT_LOAD_EXTENSION shell.c sqlite3.c


``

I think this is a serious issue, Mollie doesn't seem to be the correct party to keep a PKI bundle up-to-date at all times. To me this seems like a task for a distributor of an operating system and/or a web browser.

The solution is easy and also solves Robberts' concern: only include the strictly required CA certificates in the bundle (I assume Mollie only uses one or possibly a few CA's for their API). In that solution Mollie only needs to update the bundle when its CA hands out new root/intermediate certificates.

From my point of view, It makes sense for binary (maven) artifacts, since you are linking them with your JVM program which can be Scala version specific.

Hi Martin,

Because pip-accel's default cache invalidation scheme has always worked fine it seemed wasteful to unconditionally switch to the alternate scheme of calculating SHA1 hashes all the time (for what is essentially a quirk of AppVeyor CI, not Windows). Instead I've decided to make the cache invalidation scheme configurable.

In the newest version of pip-accel (0.36.2) the alternate cache invalidation scheme can be enabled by setting [trust_mod_times](http://pip-accel.readthedocs.org/en/latest/developers.html#pip_accel.config.Config.trust_mod_times) to `False` (this is the default on AppVeyor CI).

I hope this helps to avoid useless rebuilds. If you encounter problems feel free to reopen this issue or open a new one.

It just occurred to me that we can really cover *all* cases by having:

    replica_method =
    replica_fallback_method = 

... without making the code any more complex.  The keyword "basebackup" would mean use the built-in pg_basebackup method; anything else would need to be the name of a script.  So here's the defaults:

    replica_method = basebackup
    replica_fallback_method = none

Here's "use wal-e for large DBs, fallback to basebackup"

    replica_method = /patroni/scripts/wal-e-large.py
    replica_fallback_method = basebackup

Here's "use custom, don't fall back"

    replica_method = /patroni/scripts/san3_rsync.py
    replica_fallback_method =

Here's "use pg_basebackup, fall back to wal-e" (in case of the master being short on connections, for example):

    replica_method = basebackup
    replica_fallback_method = /patroni/scripts/wal-e.py

Make sense?

Eventually, yes :) Right now we're constrained by how we implement the map creation. It turns out to be pretty tricky to get all of cartoframes' map making constraints into a single workflow.

Short answer is: yes it's possible. Longer answer is that it will be implemented eventually, but we have to do some re-architecturing of the internals. Named maps can't template everything (https://github.com/CartoDB/Windshaft-cartodb/issues/697) and are a limited resource. So we need to figure out how to leverage anonymous maps in some cases and named maps in others (see ticket here: https://github.com/CartoDB/cartoframes/issues/205). The `future.result` from `policy.open`/`subscriber.subscribe` only resolves when the *stream* is closed. It does not resolve when the callback is executed. The callbacks are executed by a ` `ThreadPoolExecutor` that is resilient in the face of exceptions thrown by the callback (so that poorly behaved callbacks do not cause the system to crash). If you need to handle errors in your callback, you will need to add the `try:... catch:` logic there.

@DXCanas - you can see the current state of the PR here.

At a high level, I've migrated from using a few URL patterns, page names, and actions (with optional parameters that need to be carefully checked) to _many_ URL patterns, page names, and actions. I think the advantage will be that each action's set of parameters is very well-defined.

My hope is that many of the actions will share underlying API fetching code and vue files.

The primary page "types" are:
 * channel lists
 * learner lists
 * item lists
 * learner/item detail pages.

I'd like to try and consolidate logic for these various views, and then have them switch behavior slightly (e.g. internal link generation and sorting behaviors) based on whether they're being used in the Recent, Topics, or Learners sections.

----

One thing I could use your help with if you want to jump in on it today: hooking up @66eli77 's `coach-exercise-render-page` to the learner/item detail pages. This will involve:

* delete the existing `EXERCISE_RENDER` page name and route
* use `showCoachExerciseRenderPage` or some variation of it in the following actions:
  * `showRecentLearnerItemDetails`
  * `showTopicLearnerItemDetails`
  * `showLearnerItemDetails`

(note that for now, we can assume that all content detail pages are exercises - we won't handle other content types like videos on these actions/routes yet).

Thanks for the report! It seems like the error you're receiving is [produced by the API](https://developers.google.com/adwords/api/docs/common-errors#AuthenticationError.CLIENT_CUSTOMER_ID_IS_REQUIRED); i.e. the client library is making an API call and the API responds with that error message.

Generally speaking, you need to specify a client customer ID for most AdWords API calls. This client library allows you to specify that via a [configuration file](https://github.com/googleads/googleads-python-lib/blob/master/googleads.yaml) that initializes your `AdWordsClient` via the `LoadFromStorage` method. By default, this will attempt to load a configuration file named **googleads.yaml** from your home directory.

As a first step, I suggest that you verify that the `client_customer_id` optional field under `adwords` has been set.

Regards,

ok, find the problem, after having the good order, log are not good.
I've test with 2 router. (one accept flow and the other unicast)
Exabgp told me : Route added to neighbor <IP> twice with the same IP. But in the reality, It send the route to my two router.

After research, the cisco documentation say : 
The remaining 4 octets carry the rate information in IEEE floating point [IEEE.754.1985] format, units being **bytes per second**. A traffic-rate of 0 should result on all traffic for the particular flow to be discarded.

and with the command `show flowspec afi-all detail`
```
  Flow           :Dest:8.8.8.8/32,Proto:=17
    Actions      :Traffic-rate: 1600 bps  (bgp.1)
    Statistics                        (packets/bytes)
```
The unit is **bytes** too

My conclusion is that ExaBGP multiple the value by 8 because of a unit mistake

Hi @maelp , I'll answer in order.
1) That's a perfectly valid thing to want! I think it should be possible to do this without too much effort. I will try to write an example in the next few days.
2) Wether one wants to use 2D or 3D is entirely dependent on the situation I guess. The situation in research in acoustical signal processing is that many people validate their algorithms in 2D room environments. Now I think this has more to do with ease of implementation than accuracy. You can imagine that a 2D room is like a room without floor or ceiling (an infinite elevator shaft). If you are interested in realistic rendering, I would use a 3D room. Then again, the image source model is not the most life-like simulator to use. 
3) I have not used the simulator from Eric Lehmann, but if I recall correctly, it uses a more sophisticated method to improve the rendering of the tail of the RIR, but has the drawback of only handling shoebox rooms and requires Matlab.

    * Set `self._writes[key] = None`
    * By the way, it seems during state transition, `delete` method would only be triggered for `SELFDESTRUCT` or `EIP161 (Spurious Dragon)`?

3. Update the logic in `exsits`
    * Set `self._reads[key] = self.wrapped_db.get(key) if self.wrapped_db.exists(key) else None`
    * It would call `wrapped_db.get(key)` twice: one is in `self.wrapped_db.get(key)`, another is in `self.wrapped_db.exists(key)`). It seems a wasted call, but also keeping the code simple and wrapped.

Interesting, can you give some examples?  I wasn't familiar with RFC822 continuations and so I had to Google them.  From what I can tell, it seems like in things like HTTP and SMTP servers, headers can span multiple lines like so

```
From: This is a really
>From long from header.
```

Is that the kind of thing you're talking about?

FWIW, you can already have multi-line config values with triple-quoted strings in ConfigObj, e.g.

```
some_opt = '''This is a really
long config value'''
```

This accomplishes the same thing in a more Pythonesque syntax IMO.  I wouldn't necessarily reject a pull request adding RFC822 support, but it would at the very least need to be perfectly backwards-compatible, and even then I worry about the added complexity when there's already a supported approach for multiline values.

Hi @cj-anthony , underneath the CLI it is the Azure Python SDK. The model of the log settings parameter here is the `LogSettings` ([Link](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/8c792e5f1bfbc2c5fea0af12871dc11d1672c0c6/azure-mgmt-monitor/azure/mgmt/monitor/models/log_settings.py)). It is a dictionary, instead of an array.

So it would look like:
``` JSON
{
  "category": "...",
  "enable": true,
  "retention_policy" : {
    ...
  }
}
```

The monitor API had changed recently, so each diagnostic setting needs a name and has one log setting. To apply multiple log settings you need to create multiple diagnostic settings.

Here's more detail: [Azure Monitor: Enhanced capabilities for routing logs and metrics](https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/blog/azure-monitor-multiple-diagnostic-settings/).

I would close this issue for now since it is not a bug. Please feel free to leave further comments or reopen if there are other unsolved issues. Thanks.

Gunicorn spawns aiohttp workers, workers keep connections.
It basically means that worker closing terminates all TCP connections to clients.
There is no way to shut down worker with keeping it's connections, at least using gunicorn architecture.

Using an `asyncio.Semaphore` to rate limit seems to workaround this issue (`netstat` reports at or close to the limit for the duration), although I'm relatively new to `aio` python, so I'm unsure at what cost, if any.

One more thing: if we're coercing integers, shouldn't we be coercing floats too? What's the logic behind accepting one and rejecting the other?

Conversion is about type checking and coercion, but `choices` is a validation concern:

```python
>>> things.validate([2, 'a', 'foo'])
...
schematics.exceptions.CompoundError: {2: ValidationError("Value must be one of ['a', 'b'].")}
```

The difference between conversion and validation is one of the things that should be spelled out more clearly in the documentation. `ConversionError` is a subclass of `TypeError`, while `ValidationError` is a subclass of `ValueError`. Substitute `['foo']` for `'foo'` and you'll see it fail at the conversion stage.

Note that your function `func(x,y)` returns a vector instead of a scalar. Therefore, you need to use the `jacobian` function in autograd and not `grad`. Example code:
```python
import autograd.numpy as np
from autograd import jacobian

def f(x, y):
    return np.abs(x - y)

jacobian_f_wrt_x = jacobian(f, 0) # 0 indicates first input element in f(x, y)
jacobian_f_wrt_y = jacobian(f, 1) # 1 indicates second input element in f(x, y)

x = np.arange(-4, 6, 2, dtype=float)
y = np.zeros(5) + 1.5

print(jacobian_f_wrt_x(x, y))
print(jacobian_f_wrt_y(x, y))
```

Here `jacobian_f_wrt_x(x, y)` returns the partial derivative of each element in x with respect to each element in the output of `f(x, y)`. Because `x` contains 5 items and `f(x,y)` returns 5 items, this will be a 5x5 matrix. The (i,j)-th element in this matrix corresponds to the partial derivative of `output[i]` with respect to `x[j]`.

Likewise, `jacobian_f_wrt_y(x, y)` returns the partial derivative of each element in y with respect to each element in the output of `f(x,y)`.

@codinguncut , `[normalize-space()`](https://www.w3.org/TR/xpath/#function-normalize-space) only applies some trimming...:
> whitespace normalized by stripping leading and trailing whitespace and replacing sequences of whitespace characters by a single space.

...on top of `string()`, which is itself a concatenation of descendant text nodes of the context node:

> The string-value of an element node is the concatenation of the string-values of all text node descendants of the element node in document order.

And indeed, `normalize-space()` is [a string function](https://www.w3.org/TR/xpath/#section-String-Functions), whereas `//text()` means `/descendant-or-self::node()/text()`, so it selects text nodes, and does not produce a string. Two different operations.
lxml/parsel produces smart-strings out of text nodes, so they can be concatenated.

@lopuhin , @kmike , this may not be the right issue for my comment here as it may be more about doing html2text-ish transformation than plaintext, but what I found handy in the past was:
* to have an option to keep newlines, which `normalize-space()` gobbles up
* and add a newline after block elements for things like titles

You can find some (ugly, pre-knowing-of-normalize-space) [code in parslepy](https://github.com/redapple/parslepy/blob/9d256023029e486ba7a6e0a96db5d27010cb1c85/parslepy/funcs.py#L35).

Hey there, i can not show real production code, but i prepared simple working example.

Requirements:
```
dependency-injector==3.11.3
six==1.11.0
SQLAlchemy==1.2.5
```

The code:

```
import contextlib
import logging

import dependency_injector.containers as containers
import dependency_injector.providers as providers
import sqlalchemy
from sqlalchemy import orm, MetaData, Column, Integer, String, Table

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


class User:
    """Example user entity."""

    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name

    def __repr__(self):
        return f'User(name="{self.name}")'


class Database:
    """Acts as a connection pool and session factory."""

    def __init__(self, db_url):
        self.db_url = db_url
        self.engine = sqlalchemy.create_engine(db_url)
        self.session_factory = orm.scoped_session(orm.sessionmaker(bind=self.engine))

    def prepare_db(self):
        # Prepare mappings
        metadata = MetaData()
        user_table = Table(
            'user',
            metadata,
            Column('id', Integer, primary_key=True),
            Column('name', String(50)),
        )
        orm.mapper(User, user_table)

        # Create database / tables
        metadata.create_all(bind=self.engine)

        # Setup test data
        user = User(name='asyncee')
        with self.session() as s:
            s.add(user)
            s.commit()

    @contextlib.contextmanager
    def session(self):
        session = self.session_factory()
        try:
            yield session
        except Exception as e:
            logger.error('Session rollback because of exception: %s', e, exc_info=True)
            session.rollback()
        finally:
            session.close()

    @staticmethod
    def session_factory(db):
        return db.session


class UserRepository:
    def __init__(self, session):
        self.session = session

    def get_users(self):
        return self.session.query(User).all()


class UsersService:
    def __init__(self, repository):
        self.repository = repository

    def execute(self):
        return self.get_users()

    def get_users(self):
        return self.repository.get_users()


class PrintUsersUseCase:
    """Print all users in the system to stdout."""

    def __init__(self, session_factory, repository_class):
        self.session_factory = session_factory
        self.repository_class = repository_class

    def run(self):
        with self.session_factory() as session:
            # Repository or service must know only about session, because
            # many components may run in one transaction scope.
            repository = self.repository_class(session)
            users_service = UsersService(repository)
            users = users_service.execute()

            # This is business logic of this use-case.
            print(users)

            # Persist all changes into database if needed.
            session.commit()


class Core(containers.DeclarativeContainer):
    config = providers.Configuration('app_config')


class Sqla(containers.DeclarativeContainer):
    db = providers.Singleton(Database, db_url=Core.config.db_url)
    session_factory = providers.Factory(Database.session_factory, db=db)


class UseCases(containers.DeclarativeContainer):
    get_user = providers.Factory(
        GetUsersUseCase, repository_class=UserRepository,
        session_factory=Sqla.session_factory)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    Core.config.override({'db_url': 'sqlite:///:memory:'})
    Sqla.db().prepare_db()

    use_case = UseCases.get_user()
    use_case.run()
```

I'm using `session_factory` provider in this case, to hide dependency on any particular `Database` implementation.

Session factory is just a context manager, which handles exceptions automatically. If one needs to handle such situations manually, then session may be injected straight to `Repository` class as shown above in your code.

---

Currently, i did not found a way how to inject context manager and automatically run code inside (i want to avoid `with self.session_factory() as session` line in `PrintUsersUseCase.run` method. If it could be implemented, then more declarative configuration can be achieved.

I managed to demonstrate the problem that Erwin talks about in: https://stackoverflow.com/a/15950324/205832

See (but don't judge me how ugly code is): https://gist.github.com/peterbe/883da6f65e315383ca83b7ade5e94e42

When you run that you get this:
```
▶ python dummy.py
! 0
! 1
commit 1
1 page data = ('firefox_beta_58-0b15rc1_linux-i686_cs2', {}, 1523861592005, True)
commit 0
0 page data = None
```

...every time!

What it simulates is two concurrent threads. If you merge their timelines you get this:
1) (Thread 1) BEGIN
2) (Thread 2) BEGIN
3) (Thread 1) WITH create_record... 
4) (Thread 2) WITH create_record... 
5) (Thread 2) COMMIT

Basically, if you extrapolate that to the Python/kinto layer I think this happens:
```
# Thread 1
kinto_client.create('myid', 'mycollection', 'mybucket', '2018-04-16 06:53:12.004')

# Thread 2
kinto_client.create('myid', 'mycollection', 'mybucket', '2018-04-16 06:53:12.005')
```
The combination (parent_id, collection_id, last_modified) is different in both calls so you don't get trapped by the `idx_records_parent_id_collection_id_last_modified` uniqueness index on the inserts.

Thanks for catching the lack of caching on `_get_redshift_constraints`. Sounds like an easy optimization.

> When running something like alembic which does a lot of reflection, this can slow things down considerably because the dialect winds up querying information about the entire database every time alembic asks for anything

I believe there's a fundamental constraint in SQLAlchemy's API for reflection that allows caching only within the context of reflecting a single table. I originally wrote the reflection code in this dialect with bulk queries like `_get_all_relation_info` hoping that we could reconstruct the entire database schema with just a few queries, but I don't believe this is possible.

I do hope that using cache_info for the constraints query will speed things up a bit, but reflection across many tables is likely going to continue to be slow.

I solved it.
tflearn is using an older version of tensorflow .
the older api 'tf.split(axis, num_or_size_splits, value)'
now is 'tf.split(value, num_or_size_splits, axis)'
so you should go to    '/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/tflearn/layers/recurrent.py", line 601' find the function 'array_ops.split(....)'  and change the location of 'value' and 'axis'

Note also that as @lvdmaaten mentioned, you can use `_send` directly with the usual Plotly API.

Specifically, for date/time xaxis, see https://plot.ly/python/time-series/ ('Time Series Plot with Custom Date Range'):

```
import plotly.plotly as py
import plotly.graph_objs as go

import datetime

def to_unix_time(dt):
    epoch =  datetime.datetime.utcfromtimestamp(0)
    return (dt - epoch).total_seconds() * 1000

x = [datetime.datetime(year=2013, month=10, day=04),
    datetime.datetime(year=2013, month=11, day=05),
    datetime.datetime(year=2013, month=12, day=06)]
data = [dict(
            x=x,
            y=[1, 3, 6])]

layout = dict(xaxis = dict(
                   range = [to_unix_time(datetime.datetime(2013, 10, 17)),
                            to_unix_time(datetime.datetime(2013, 11, 20))]
    ))

```
Note that you don't need to use `go.Scatter` and `go.Layout` (you can use `dict`s instead).

self._send({
        'data': data,
         'win': win,
         'eid': env,
         'layout': layout
 })
```

Hi @kootenpv,

As pointed by @apiguy, the current tokenizer used by fastText is extremely simple: it considers white-spaces as token boundaries. It is thus highly recommended to preprocess the data before feeding it to fastText (e.g. tokenization, lowercasing, etc).

We might add more options for text normalization in the future, but we do not have a timeline yet.

@poppingtonic It may be sufficient for your purposes, but you can easily do much better. This question was asked here before, but I forget by whom. As far as I know there is no concrete answer from Facebook on how the preprocessing was done :(

What I can tell you though is that the pre-trained models in the repository were **NOT** created with the provided [``wikifil.pl``](https://github.com/facebookresearch/fastText/blob/master/wikifil.pl) script. The ``wikifil.pl`` is not very sophisticated, it will strip all special characters from your input and leave you only with lower case letters from ``a-z``. This will give you worse results when compared to the pre-trained binaries provided -- which are unicode and include special characters. For English ``wikifil.pl` might be OK for your purposes but other languages will suffer depending on the degree that they make use of special characters. 

In regards to using the ``enwik9`` archive please know that it is a bit small in size and will not even come close to the quality of the pre-trained vectors which have been generated from a recent and whole wikipedia dump (which is about 10-13GB compressed and 60GB uncompressed).

That said, if you are serious about making your own vectors based on wikipedia you simply need to get a recent wiki dump available here: [https://dumps.wikimedia.org/enwiki/latest/enwiki-latest-pages-articles.xml.bz2](https://dumps.wikimedia.org/enwiki/latest/enwiki-latest-pages-articles.xml.bz2) 

If you are using Linux or macOS you can pipe the output from ``bzip2`` directly into your preprocessor script of choice meaning that you won't have to unpack the archive at all. A good wiki preprocessor script which will do a much better job for most languages is available here: [https://github.com/attardi/wikiextractor](https://github.com/attardi/wikiextractor).

I have an initial stab at what I'm thinking with the revamp of embedded python scripting. You can see it in the [pyscript](https://github.com/python-cmd2/cmd2/tree/pyscript) branch.

You can try it out with the subcommand and tab completion examples.

Run the example of your choice, and enter the interactive python shell (`py`)
Everything is accessible through `app` or `cmd`
Every top-level command is accessible as a function on `app` or `cmd`. Each layer of sub-command can be called as a function on the return value of the previous command function. Each of the arguments or flags can be called as function arguments.

For example, if you try `tab_autocompletion.py` each of these is a valid way to execute a command:

> Philosophical question here: Can I ask what the actual intent is in delineating holder between copyable and move-only, rather than keeping it more towards shared and unique?

It's not really a philosophical question, it's just that we have fundamentally different code paths when a holder can be copied, with the `copyable_` and `move_only_` base classes capturing the different implementations.  Essentially, we *are* already basically assuming that copyable means shared ownership and move-only implies unique ownership.

But more generally (and the thing that affects backwards compatibility) is that we allow `struct holder_helper` specializations to be used to adapt any random smart pointer type that someone has on hand, but that such a specialization isn't needed if the holder type has a `get()` method that returns the pointer.

Initial analysis:

The reason why this happens is that the optimized implementation of the `Cpc.dpm_enabled` property assumes that the `Cpc.partitions.list()` method would raise an HTTP error in case the CPC is in classic mode. That is the case for EC12 (where there are no partitions whatsoever, so it raises 404,1 "resource not found"), but not for z13. On z13, the "List Partitions of CPC" operation succeeds with no returned partitions when the CPC is in classic mode. This is also explicitly stated in the description of that operation in the HMC API book.

Options:
1. Fall back to the simple implementation of looking at the `dpm-enabled` resource property of the CPC. This would cause a longer elapsed time when accessing that property, compared to the current implementation. On a second access of the Cpc Python resource object, the value would already be there (and under the usual user-controlled currency management).

Also, using the fix suggested by @impala2 [here](https://github.com/python-social-auth/social-core/issues/111#issue-240366048) is a bit broken too. The authorization endpoint is creating new token always, even though it is not required.

@aldanor , there are two sides to your question w.r.t. support for dynamic resource requirements: accounting for them and enforcing them. Accounting should be straightforward. This basically involves changing the Task's resource requirement vector at runtime. I don't see any fundamental difficulty with doing that. Once changed (e.g., updating MEMREQ from, say 128MB to 512MB), the task will be scheduled in accordance with its latest known task spec. The second part, enforcement is orthogonal and is a little more involved. In the simplest case, it would require python workers to set rlimits on memory for each task that comes with such a memory limit requirement. 
Question is, can you think of any use cases other than memory where updating resource requirements dynamically would make sense?

actor1 may need to create several actors, and those actors may be needed by remote functions. 

The structure of the code is such that there is a parent actor that contains several handles to several different actors. actor1 acts as the manager for these inner actors. New actors can be built at any time. To avoid having to create an ActorMethod on actor1 for each method that operates on these inner actors, or to operate on them directly, we need a way to get them out of actor1.

For now, the easy fix is to just make the outermost actor (actor1) not be an actor. The consequences of this are probably a loss of asynchrony.

@Pike 
None of the profiling I've done has looked at the parser at all. For my use case (generating translations in a Django app, see [django-ftl](https://django-ftl.readthedocs.io/en/latest/) ), it's of very little interest, because the parsing step (and compiling to Python step added in this branch) are one-time costs that are done at application startup.

So all of my benchmarks so far have focussed on the runtime performance of the compiled code. In many cases, the code is extremely minimal (e.g. returning a constant for the simple case - https://github.com/projectfluent/python-fluent/pull/71/files#diff-8ea7f46014c70a54a5d42179849a3a5aR56 ). For more complex cases, it is dominated by things like number formatting, or the plural rules lookup (which uses [Babel's implementation](http://babel.pocoo.org/en/latest/api/plural.html#babel.plural.to_python) and can be quite expensive for complicated rules). 

The benchmark script I've added could be extended to do benchmarks of the parser though.

Yes, this has come up a few times, and it's kind of both trio and pylint's fault: pylint is using imperfect heuristics to try to guess what members are exported from a module, and trio's exports are put together in a complicated way that pylint's heuristics don't work on. There are similar issues with other tools that try to analyze trio statically (like mypy, pycharm, etc.), rather than at runtime (like ipython).

Unfortunately we can't really fix all those tools, so we should change how trio does exports. We have an open issue for that: #542. No-one's started working on it yet, but I'm sure it will happen sometime... or if it's something you wanted to work on then speak up there or in chat and we can help you figure out how to get started.

Update: there have been other issues and discussions related to this, I think the ideal approach is to remove Tornado dependency completely and implement async span flushing in a dedicated thread. The thread should also be started lazily (e.g. only when the first span is reported), which should address two issues:
  * when running under WSGI-style frameworks, the thread will naturally be started post-fork
  * if the thread is always started asynchronously (unlike the threadloop which currently blocks), then it won't be causing the deadlocks
